q: Summarize the book of Matthew: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Matthew (Levi) the apostle, a former tax collector; c. A.D. 50–70. Written primarily for a Jewish-Christian audience, it presents Jesus as the promised Messiah and King.

**Genre.** Gospel.

**Theme.** Jesus is the long-awaited King of the Jews and royal Son of David who fulfills Old Testament prophecy and inaugurates the kingdom of heaven. Matthew repeatedly shows Scripture "fulfilled" in Christ.

**Outline.**
- 1–4 — Birth, infancy, and beginning of ministry
- 5–7 — The Sermon on the Mount
- 8–18 — Galilean ministry, miracles, and teaching
- 19–25 — Journey to Jerusalem and Olivet Discourse
- 26–28 — Passion, resurrection, and Great Commission

**Christ & significance.** Matthew portrays Jesus as the kingly Messiah and new Moses; the closing Great Commission sends the church to disciple all nations under His authority.
summary: Matthew the apostle, c. A.D. 50–70. A Jewish-oriented Gospel presenting Jesus as the promised Messiah-King who fulfills prophecy and commissions the church.

q: Summarize the book of Mark: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** John Mark, recording the eyewitness preaching of the apostle Peter; c. A.D. 50–65, likely from Rome for a Gentile (Roman) audience.

**Genre.** Gospel.

**Theme.** Jesus is the suffering Servant and powerful Son of God who comes to give His life as a ransom for many. Fast-paced and action-driven, Mark stresses what Jesus does more than long discourses.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Preparation and call of disciples
- 1–8 — Galilean ministry, miracles, and growing conflict
- 8–10 — Way to Jerusalem and passion predictions
- 11–13 — Jerusalem ministry and Olivet Discourse
- 14–16 — Passion, death, and resurrection

**Christ & significance.** Mark presents Jesus as the active, authoritative Servant-King whose mission climaxes at the cross; the Servant "came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many" (10:45).
summary: John Mark, drawing on Peter's preaching, c. A.D. 50–65. A brisk, action-focused Gospel showing Jesus as the powerful Servant who gives His life as a ransom.

q: Summarize the book of Luke: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Luke, the physician and companion of Paul (also author of Acts); c. A.D. 60–62. An orderly account addressed to Theophilus, written with a Gentile readership in view.

**Genre.** Gospel.

**Theme.** Jesus is the compassionate Savior of all people who seeks and saves the lost. Luke highlights the poor, women, outcasts, and Gentiles, and emphasizes prayer, the Holy Spirit, and joy.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Birth and infancy narratives
- 3–4 — Preparation and inauguration of ministry
- 4–9 — Galilean ministry
- 9–19 — Travel narrative toward Jerusalem
- 19–24 — Passion, resurrection, and ascension

**Christ & significance.** Luke portrays Jesus as the perfect Son of Man and universal Savior whose grace reaches the marginalized; "the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost" (19:10).
summary: Luke the physician, Paul's companion, c. A.D. 60–62. An orderly Gospel for Gentiles presenting Jesus as the compassionate Savior of all who seeks the lost.

q: Summarize the book of John: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** John the apostle, the "beloved disciple"; c. A.D. 85–95, likely from Ephesus. Distinct from the Synoptics, it is a theological Gospel written so readers may believe.

**Genre.** Gospel.

**Theme.** Jesus is the eternal, divine Son of God, the Word made flesh, who gives eternal life to all who believe. John structures his account around signs and the great "I am" sayings to reveal Christ's deity.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Prologue: the Word made flesh
- 1–12 — Book of Signs: public ministry and miracles
- 13–17 — Upper Room discourse and high-priestly prayer
- 18–20 — Passion and resurrection
- 21 — Epilogue: restoration of Peter

**Christ & significance.** John uniquely presents Jesus as the pre-existent divine Son and great "I am"; his purpose is that readers "may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God" and have life in His name (20:31).
summary: John the apostle, c. A.D. 85–95. A theological Gospel built on signs and "I am" sayings, presenting Jesus as the divine Son so readers may believe and have life.

q: Summarize the book of Acts: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Luke, the physician and companion of Paul, writing the sequel to his Gospel; c. A.D. 62, again addressed to Theophilus.

**Genre.** History.

**Theme.** The risen Christ, through the outpoured Holy Spirit, builds and expands His church from Jerusalem outward to the ends of the earth. Acts traces the unstoppable spread of the gospel across cultural and geographic barriers.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Ascension and Pentecost
- 3–7 — The church in Jerusalem
- 8–12 — Witness in Judea, Samaria, and to the Gentiles
- 13–20 — Paul's missionary journeys
- 21–28 — Paul's arrest, trials, and journey to Rome

**Christ & significance.** Acts shows the ascended Christ ruling by His Spirit, fulfilling 1:8 as the gospel advances from Jerusalem to Rome through Spirit-empowered witnesses despite persecution.
summary: Luke, c. A.D. 62, as the sequel to his Gospel. The Spirit-empowered spread of the church from Jerusalem to Rome, fulfilling Christ's commission in Acts 1:8.
